speaker: interviewer
text: What other changes were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the bombing stopped, the armies went through the city, continue going-- occupied the country, After the country was occupied, the trouble began.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in any ghettos?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto didn't begin right away. First, they came into the city, and established a Jewish city hall. They separated the Jews from the Gentiles, and established a Jewish city hall. They elected a Jewish president, a respectable man, a doctor. Moshe Pelz-- M-O-S-H-E, Moshe, Pelz, P-E-L-Z-- as president, plus another 10 respectable citizens from the city. And they established a Jewish city hall, and this was in contact with the Nazis. This was the contact people, and they are where the trouble begin. Every few days they brought in a new law. First law was that all the Jews have to deliver the gold and the silver-- the gold, and the silver, and the diamond to the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Jewish president gave an order to the Jewish people to deliver, and whoever had something delivered-- some did deliver, some hid-- and most people delivered whatever they had. And this was going on and on and on. New regulation, new laws every week. Suffering after suffering. Then they gave an order, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they called on all the intellectual people-- political leaders, organization leaders, community leaders. They arrested them. They send them away to Auschwitz, and they never returned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this affect anyone in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did not affect my family, but affect my friends. Yes, I knew all those people who they sent away. It's a fact that some of those people even survived the war, and they live today in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family do as these laws came out? Did they turn in their gold and their valuables?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some pieces we delivered. Some pieces were hidden, we didn't-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they hidden?

speaker: interviewee
text: In an attic, in the ground, in the backyard. Some objects are hidden until today, in Kielce, in our building, that I never recouped. It's still in the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your life change in any other way because of these laws? You were working with your dad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure, life change. Life changed quite a bit, yeah. As this went on and on and on and on and on, little by little, they establish a ghetto. They separate us completely from the Gentile people. They establish factories, mostly lumber mills, and everybody went to work to the lumber mills. And we worked for the German people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what did this mean for you, your family had a lumber mill.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our lumber mill was taken over by the Germans. And we worked in our own company, we worked manufacturing certain things. Lumber-- lumber things for the German people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became a laborer.

speaker: interviewee
text: We became a laborer for the German people. They brought in a commissioner, a commissioner who took over all the business. And we worked in our own business for the German people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you move, also have to move where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the factory was in the back of our building, two or three blocks away. Yeah, the whole neighborhood was factories, and we worked in the neighborhood until a certain time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you continued to live in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: We continued to live--

speaker: interviewer
text: But your work you now worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factories were in the ghetto. The factories were in the ghetto. Everybody worked for the Germans. Yeah, for nothing in the ghetto, right, right. And this was going on and on and on, the suffering on and on and on, until the year 1942-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where the Nazis liquidated the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that involve?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Nazis liquidate the ghetto, they surrounded the ghetto with thousands of police with machine guns, and everybody had to get out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On a certain place, without clothing, without suitcases, without belongings. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They segregated men separate, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] children separate, and young people separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in the group of young people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I were with the group of young people, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So the-- the men, they put on trains, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and also the women and the children. And shipped them away to Treblinka-- Treblinka, Poland. And from there, nobody ever returned. After [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the ghetto was liquidated, they took about 1,500 young people, and they put them in a small ghetto. And from there, we went to work in different factories all around the city. Myself with my brothers, we still went back to our own factory, and our own address. The factory did exist, yeah. We'd been living in this small ghetto for quite a while, and every morning we went to work in groups-- to our place, to another place, to Huta Ludwikow, to Henrykow, to different places, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] for a long period of time, while the war was going on. After living in the small ghetto for a period of time-- this was sometimes in end of 1943-- they decided to liquidate the small ghetto. One morning, we woke up, they surrounded the ghetto with police, took out all the people, and segregated again. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] Segregated again. The president of this small ghetto, with all his children, were taken out in the Jewish cemetery. And they build their own grave, a round grave. They told them to surround the grave, and after the grave was already dug, they took machine guns and killed them-- and everybody fell into the grave. They had several people behind, that they covered the grave, and they left. Next morning, the Polish people went to look to see what happened, and they saw the grave. The soil moved. The people were buried alive. This was the end of the Jewish community with the president of Kielce. His name was Hermann Levy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how do you know that this is what was done, and how the Polish people reacted?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish people lived around the cemetery-- in 20 feet of the cemetery. They looked out the windows and saw what's going on, and some of the people even recognized Mr. Hermann Levy. He was the most respectable business people in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you learn about what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that happen? You were in camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I were-- I were in the camp. I were in the camp, and after this happened, they segregated in the small camp, all the people. Out of the 1,500 people, 900 went Huta Ludwikow to work, and Henrykow, and two camps in Kielce. Me and my brothers went on a transport from Kielce, by truck, to Pionki, Poland. It was the largest manufacturing of ammunition in Poland. While going to Pionki by truck, several people jumped the trucks, and the police killed them. We stopped in a forest for a rest for 15 minutes. I had a gun and a grenade in my pocket, so I took and hid it in the forest, under a tree. I dig it in, in the soil. I said to myself, this isn't the end-- Pionki-- I will escape, I will return and continue my life someplace else. When we arrived in Pionki, we went to work to manufacture ammunition. Life was very, very hard. And very bitter. My oldest brother worked in one part of the factory. My other brother worked in another part of the factory. My friend worked in a different section. I worked [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a section where we used to make NC-- nitroglycerin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dangerous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Used to-- every two or three days, we used to have explosions. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Lots of people couldn't breathe from the nitroglycerin. And myself, I saw that this is no future. Long I'm not going to live here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you've got to figure how to get out from this hell. My department wasn't too far away from the fence. I start observing the fence every day, the way the patrols used to patrol the fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every half hour, one patrol went to the right, and the other patrol went to the left. I had half an hour time between. As the patrol passed, one went to the right, and one to the left. Was 30 minutes timed-- it was timed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was already established. Second, I'd been counting how many barbed wires are on the fence, and how many electric wires. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Every 24 inches was one electric wire. Three barbed wires, and one electric wire. Then I figured out completely-- with friends of mine-- how many electric wires are-- I say, now is the time to go. How did you know that they were electric wires?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we knew, we could see it was electric. We knew that people who tried to escape got electrocuted. I saw, many times, people escaping, and they'd been hanging on the wires. We figured out pretty good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one night, a couple people decided to go. I knew those people. They went first, and it was quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went next. I walked over, straight from work-- I worked night shift-- straight from work to the wires. I had some rocks in my shoes, and slowly I climbed the wire. I was young and peppy, and I jumped. Successfully, I cross, and I went down.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came on the other side of the wires, I looked around. Which way to go? Roughly, I made my own map, and I decided to go a certain way from the camp, on the way of Kielce. I didn't know exactly the map, but I figured I'd go near the railroad train.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're roughly one kilometer from the railroad train, I will follow the train. In this way, I find my way. And that's what I did. While I escaped, after one kilometer walking, it was probably one in the morning. Fire start coming near me, heavy automatic fire. I say, oh, I am in trouble-- this is police.

speaker: interviewee
text: What can a person do when they're attacked? I saw a corn field. I ran into the corn field, I bend down, on all four-- on the hand and on the feet-- I was-- I was going slowly, slowly, slowly. I made maybe about two or three kilometers.

speaker: interviewee
text: The fire subsided. And I got up on my feet, and I saw it was quiet. And I start marching according my map.

speaker: interviewee
text: And turned out, it was successful. I marched [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by 60 kilometer, and on the way was a Jewish camp, in Radom. And I knew some people in this camp, so I said, why should I go all the way to Kielce? I take a rest and find out what's going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went over, and they had a fence-- looked around. I found a good spot, I jumped over the fence, and I went into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you say you jumped over the fence, it was a low lying?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably 8 feet-- 8 feet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not electric?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no-- electric. It was a labor camp. I went into the camp there. I knew some people there. I stayed there in the camp about eight, 10 days. I rested up, I recuperated a little bit over there. There was a little more to eat. And nobody said nothing. And after several days, I was on my way. I went out from Radom, and I went by 30 kilometers on the way of Kielce. And I looked for the place where I unloaded my gun. I had a very good mark-- it was near an electric pole, with huge transformers. And there was a certain number, a big number. And according to numbers, I found-- the-- the electric pole with the transformers, I went there, nearby, and I started digging. Didn't took me more than one minute, and I found it. I checked it out, was in good condition. I took my grenade-- I had one grenade, one gun, and five bullets. When I bought the gun, I had six bullets. I couldn't get any more bullets. Before I paid for the gun, I tested one bullet, to make sure if I wasn't cheated. It worked, I paid for that, and I only have five bullets. So the five bullets were dear to me. I couldn't use them-- I have to have them for the last moment, so I saved them. I picked up the gun, and I went on the march of Kielce. And slowly, I didn't go too fast. I made five, 10, 15 kilometers. But slowly, I arrived in Kielce. When I arrived in Kielce, I didn't arrive through the city. I went through the forest, through the fields. And the city was located-- the camp was located on the outskirts of the city-- Huta Ludwikow. I camp near the camp. I walked over there and waited until the people come home from work, 5 o'clock.

speaker: interviewee
text: While they've been going home from work, I jumped over the fence, and went into the camp. And then I came into the camp, they start asking questions. How? What? Where? Where is everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody want to know how their relatives are. Oh, and I had a good memory. And I told them the whole story. And they accepted me. They accepted me, but they were afraid of the president of the camp. He was a German. His name was Mr. Fuss, F-U-S-S. He was a very good leader, Mr. Fuss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, he was a German Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- no, he was a German. A real German. A leader of the SS, of the high echelon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was like a commandant.

speaker: interviewee
text: A commandant, yeah. He ran the camp, and they were afraid of him. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was a wheeler-dealer. He believed in woman, a womanizer, he liked lots of diamonds, he liked gold, American dollar, jewelry. So he was bribed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the commandant of the camp, wasn't too afraid for him. But he couldn't trust them either. So when I came into the camp, I was, for the first two weeks, illegal.

speaker: interviewee
text: After two weeks, a respectable policemen of the camp escaped. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His name [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was Proszowski-- P-R-O-S-Z-O-W-S-K-I. He escaped. And the chief of the camp was afraid to notify that somebody escaped from the camp. A respectable guy of the guard of the camp. So they didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So decided not to notify nobody. Used-- that the other guy-- they gave me Proszowski Rubin a name. They put me in his bed, and everything was quiet and peaceful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's all. And I replaced the other guy. The other guy went away to the forest, with some of my friends, hiding. He was in a bunker, not far from Kielce. And while he was in the bunker several weeks, that guy who was hiding him went in that night, killed them and took him away the money. And this was the end of Proszowski Rubin, a very good friend of mine. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was living in the camp. Went to work. I was manufacturing spokes for wheels. They supposed to use those wheels to march to Russia, to go occupy Russia. I worked there, I knew all the people there, I had lots of friends there. The people of the camp received me very well-- very well. I came home poor, empty, I didn't have one cent. And I had some guys who knew my father. They respected me very well, and they helped me a lot, yeah. And I lived there in the camp, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] mostly at the time of the war, till late date in 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In 1944, it was sometimes early September. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, no, no, no, no-- no, no, no-- while I was in the camp working in Huta Ludwikow, the uprising, the Warsaw ghetto began. And we'd been getting reports every day that the Jews are shipped to Treblinka from Warsaw. This is 100 miles outside Warsaw, in the deep forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Treblinka or Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Treblinka-- Treblinka is 100 miles outside Warsaw, in the deep, deep forest. That's one shipment, the other shipment, third shipment went to Treblinka. They found out, and they decided-- they decided in Warsaw to rebel. And they start rebelling. They didn't obey the German order. The German came in one day, asked for 10,000 people. They say no. Came in the next day for 5,000 people, they refuse, and start to go on fighting. And they start to rebel, day by day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know? You were in a camp at the time, correct? How did you know what was happening in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland had lots of underground. People-- Jews and non-Jews-- were in the underground. Where several Jews living in the forest, and they had radio, listening to England, and to other countries in Europe. And they also have connection with espionage. I didn't have no connection, but they had. Because we had, every day, news. Coming in, news to the camp, every day. And whatever came in, it was true. Some trains went through Kielce, and we had, in the factory, a ramp. And some guys used to throw out notes, to notify us what was going on. Some transport used to go through the city. They used to throw out notes, what's going on. So we used to get news, and the news used to check out good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while the revolt was going on in Warsaw, every day they attacked the camp and took out 500, 600, 800. They took them to the Umschlagplatz in Warsaw. This was in the middle of the city, a railroad station, where they used to ship them to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as they arrived, two or three hours later, they were gassed and burned. They used to throw them in, in graves, and burn them.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is what you heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, what I heard from people, reliable people who saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who told you? Adults?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adults-- adults, people, and people who used to live in Treblinka, surrounded Treblinka. Two blocks, three blocks away from the camp. Is villagers, farmers. They used to see it, and they used to tell it to people. And people used to come and tell us the story. And we checked it out, and everything was truth. So they'd been fighting the Jews in the ghetto, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe between six and 10 weeks. Every day, they conquer three buildings, four buildings, five buildings. Day by day, day by day. Then they decided they couldn't take the camp, so they went with tanks. They attacked the camps with tanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first day, the Jews killed between 30 and 60 Germans. Soon they killed 60 Germans, they put the ghetto on fire. From every corner of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: They put fire bombs, and put the whole ghetto on fire. The whole ghetto went in flames. Jews used to live in the sewer system, in the basement in the building. Soon they start choking. They start escaped. According statistic, several thousand Jews escaped alive. And they went to the Aryan side. And quite a few of them did survive. And the rest were burned, a few they captured. They delivered them to Treblinka, and they burned until the last one, and they conquered the Warsaw ghetto. This was the liquidation of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now by this time, you had been in a number of camps. You had escaped--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still in the camp in Kielce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You'd escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still in the camp in Kielce, and listened to all the rumors. While the Warsaw Jews were shipped to Treblinka, from Warsaw, two of our Kielce Jews escaped. One of them was the name Yosef Wasser-- W-A-S-S-E-R. The other one was by the name of Moinek Mydlo. M-O-I-N-E-K, Mydlo-- M-Y-D-L-O. Both escaped, both came to my city. We took them into the small camp. They told us the whole story. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They told us the story, and they live with us. After a few days, after a few weeks, the Russian armies start to push towards Poland. Then the new trouble began.
